title: Roast spatchcock chicken
subtitle: Backbone removed, flattened, roasted in 45 minutes
excerpt: A whole chicken with the backbone removed so it lies flat in the roasting tin, which cuts the cooking time by a third and means the skin crisps all over rather than just on top.
sourceNotes: Spatchcock technique is ancient; the term appears in eighteenth-century British cookbooks. The method is experiencing renewed popularity in modern British home cooking.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Spatchcock and marinate up to 24 hours ahead. Keep uncovered in the fridge for drier, crispier skin.

glossaryTerms.definition: Removing the backbone from a whole bird so it lies completely flat. This shortens the roasting time and allows the skin to crisp evenly across the entire surface.
Leaving the cooked chicken to stand for 10 minutes before carving, allowing the juices to redistribute evenly through the breast and legs.

content.text: Spatchcocking a chicken takes two minutes with kitchen scissors and transforms both the cooking time and the result. A whole chicken in a regular oven takes 90 minutes; spatchcocked, it takes 45. More usefully, the flat shape means the thighs and breasts finish at almost the same time, and the entire skin surface can brown rather than just the top.
Place the chicken breast-side down on a board. Use strong kitchen scissors to cut along both sides of the backbone from the tail end to the neck. Remove the backbone entirely.
is complete when the chicken lies completely flat: press firmly down on the breastbone with the heel of your hand until you feel it crack and the bird flattens.

Mix the olive oil, garlic, thyme, and lemon zest together. Rub all over the chicken, including under the breast skin where possible. Season generously with salt. Leave at room temperature for 30 minutes if time allows.

Heat the oven to 220 degrees C fan. Place the flattened chicken skin-side up in a roasting tin. Tuck the lemon halves around it. Roast for 40 to 45 minutes until the skin is deeply golden and crisp throughout.
Check that the thigh reads 75 degrees C.

items.symptom: The backbone will not cut with kitchen scissors
items.cause: The scissors are too small, or the chicken is very large and cold.
items.fix: Use the largest kitchen scissors you have. Cut in two stages: trim from the tail up one side, then from the neck down the other. A boning knife works as a backup.

items.symptom: The chicken is still not flat after pressing on the breastbone
items.cause: Not enough force, or the bird is large and the breast cartilage is strong.
items.fix: Use the heel of both hands together and press down firmly in the centre of the breast. The cartilage will crack. This is correct.

items.symptom: The skin is crisp at the top but pale on the thighs
items.cause: The thighs were not fully flattened and the skin did not contact the air.
items.fix: Ensure the bird is truly flat: the thighs should sit skin-side up with no folding under. Use a heavy tin that conducts heat well.

items.symptom: The breast is dry while the legs are still undercooked
items.cause: The bird was very uneven in thickness: a very large breast and small legs.
items.fix: For a very large bird, insert a meat thermometer in the thigh before roasting. The thigh temperature is the guide, not timing.

content.text: A yoghurt and harissa marinade gives a more vibrant result. For a plainer roast, season with just salt and lemon and roast at a slightly lower temperature. The backbone can be saved and used to make stock.

The word spatchcock appears in Irish and British cooking references from the eighteenth century, though the technique is older. It fell largely out of fashion in domestic British cooking through the twentieth century. It has returned as a weeknight roast technique over the last twenty years, popularised by food writers looking for a faster, more reliably cooked roast chicken.
